message.content:
{
  "materials": {
    "ppt": {
      "deckMode": "teacher-delivery",
      "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
      "templateName": "Kamalaniketan Session PPT Template",
      "themeId": "kamalaniketan-modern",
      "title": "Integrated Science: Assessment & Revision",
      "presentationTitle": "Integrated Science: Comprehensive Review and Application",
      "presentationGoal": "To consolidate core scientific concepts, reinforce practical skills, and assess student understanding through targeted revision activities.",
      "audience": "Middle School Integrated Science Students (Grades 6-8)",
      "theme": "kamalaniketan-modern",
      "themeTokens": {
        "fonts": {
          "heading": "Outfit",
          "body": "Inter"
        },
        "colors": {
          "primary": "#36ADAA",
          "secondary": "#1EABDA",
          "accent": "#DE8431",
          "background": "#EEF4F7",
          "surface": "#FFFFFF",
          "text": "#0F172A",
          "mutedText": "#64748B"
        },
        "visualStyle": {
          "topBarStyle": "bold color band",
          "cardStyle": "rounded modern educator cards",
          "visualFrameStyle": "large clean visual panel"
        }
      },
      "assetSearchPlan": {
        "preferredSources": [
          "Internal SVG",
          "Google Imagen API"
        ],
        "safeSearch": true,
        "licensingNotes": [
          "Use internally generated images and in-app SVG diagrams only."
        ],
        "fallbackStrategy": "Prefer SVG diagrams for concept/process slides and the Google Imagen API for all picture-based visuals."
      },
      "licenseChecklist": [
        "Review generated visuals for classroom accuracy before final export.",
        "Ensure scientific terminology in labels is accurate to current curriculum standards."
      ],
      "presentationWarnings": [
        "Do not include untaught future-session content.",
        "Focus on conceptual connections rather than isolated facts during revision."
      ],
      "coverageSummary": {
        "learningOutcomesCovered": [
          "LO1: Recall and apply core scientific principles across Physics, Chemistry, Biology, and Earth Science",
          "LO2: Interpret data from graphs, diagrams, and experimental results",
          "LO3: Demonstrate understanding of the Scientific Method through inquiry-based questions"
        ],
        "topicsCovered": [
          "Scientific Inquiry & Observation",
          "Matter and Its Properties",
          "Forces and Motion",
          "Living Organisms and Environment",
          "Earth Systems"
        ],
        "taughtConceptsCovered": [
          "Data interpretation skills",
          "Cause-and-effect reasoning in experiments",
          "Cross-disciplinary connections (e.g., energy flow)",
          "Practical safety protocols"
        ],
        "omittedContent": []
      },
      "slides": [
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "title_identity",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Integrated Science: Review Session 1",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 1,
          "slideType": "hook | concept | comparison | process | summary",
          "slideTitle": "Science in Action: A Comprehensive Review",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO1"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Integrated Science Overview"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "To set the stage for a high-energy revision session and remind students of their progress.",
          "studentTakeaway": "I am ready to connect what I've learned across different science disciplines today.",
          "layout": "Title + Hero Visual",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Subject: Integrated Science",
            "Session Type: Assessment & Revision",
            "Focus: Connecting Concepts"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Integrated Science Review",
            "Connecting Physics, Chemistry, Biology & Earth Science",
            "Ready to Explore?"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Welcome everyone! Today is a special session where we bring together everything we've learned.",
            "We aren't just memorizing facts; we are seeing how the world works as one connected system.",
            "Ask students: 'What was your favorite experiment so far?' to get them engaged immediately.",
            "Transition: Let's look at what we aim to achieve today."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": "A dynamic collage or concept map showing icons of a microscope, atom, planet, and force vector connected by lines.",
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Concept map connecting Biology (microscope), Chemistry (atom), Physics (force arrow), Earth Science (planet) to show integration.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual representation of integrated science",
              "searchQuery": "integrated science concept art education icons microscope atom planet force vector connected",
              "sourceSite": "Google Imagen API",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Internally generated or Creative Commons",
              "attributionText": "Educational Concept Art",
              "altText": "Illustration showing the connection between different branches of science.",
              "placementHint": "Right panel / Full bleed background"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Fade in title and bullet points sequentially."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 2
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "learning_outcomes",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Today's Learning Goals",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 2,
          "slideType": "concept | summary",
          "slideTitle": "What We Will Achieve Today",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO1",
            "LO2",
            "LO3"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Learning Objectives"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "To clearly state the measurable outcomes so students know exactly what success looks like.",
          "studentTakeaway": "I understand that I need to be able to explain concepts, read data, and think scientifically by the end of class.",
          "layout": "3-Column Outcome Cards",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Apply core principles across disciplines",
            "Interpret graphs and experimental data accurately",
            "Demonstrate scientific inquiry skills"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "1. Apply Core Principles: Connect Physics, Chemistry & Biology concepts.",
            "2. Data Interpretation: Read graphs, diagrams, and results with confidence.",
            "3. Scientific Inquiry: Use evidence to answer complex questions."
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "By the end of this session, you should be able to do three main things.",
            "First, apply what you know across different topics—like how energy moves in biology and physics.",
            "Second, look at a graph or diagram and explain exactly what it tells us without guessing.",
            "Third, use evidence-based reasoning just like real scientists when solving problems.",
            "These are the skills that will help you on your exams and in the real world."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": null,
          "visualPlan": "Three distinct cards or icons representing 'Application', 'Data Analysis', and 'Inquiry'.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Iconography for learning outcomes",
              "searchQuery": "science education icon application data analysis inquiry magnifying glass graph brain connected dots",
              "sourceSite": "Google Imagen API",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Internally generated or Creative Commons",
              "attributionText": "Learning Outcome Icons",
              "altText": "Icons representing application, data analysis, and inquiry.",
              "placementHint": "Below each bullet point"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Slide in outcome cards one by one."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 3
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "prerequisite_knowledge",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Recall: What We Know Already",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 3,
          "slideType": "concept | summary",
          "slideTitle": "Building on Our Foundation",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO1"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Prerequisite Knowledge"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "To activate prior knowledge and identify any gaps before diving into complex revision.",
          "studentTakeaway": "I can recall key terms and basic concepts from previous sessions that are essential for today's review.",
          "layout": "Recall Grid / Question Cards",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Key vocabulary: Matter, Energy, Force, Ecosystems",
            "Basic laws of motion and chemical reactions",
            "Structure-function relationships in living things"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Recall Key Terms:",
            "- Matter & Its States",
            "- Forces & Motion",
            "- Living Systems",
            "Quick Check: What is the unit of Force?",
            "Answer: Newton (N)"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Before we go deeper, let's check our memory banks.",
            "We've talked about matter, forces, and living systems. Can anyone remind us what a 'Newton' measures? It's force!",
            "Think back to the chemical reactions lab—what were the signs that a reaction occurred?",
            "This session builds on these basics; if you remember them well, today will be much easier.",
            "Transition: Now let's spark our curiosity with a real-world scenario."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": null,
          "visualPlan": "A word cloud or grid of key terms from previous sessions (Matter, Energy, Force, Ecosystem).",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual aid for vocabulary recall",
              "searchQuery": "science keywords word cloud matter energy force ecosystem education background",
              "sourceSite": "Google Imagen API",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Internally generated or Creative Commons",
              "attributionText": "Vocabulary Cloud",
              "altText": "A visual representation of key science vocabulary.",
              "placementHint": "Center background"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Pop up terms as they are discussed."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 3
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "lesson_hook",
          "templateSlideTitle": "The Mystery of the Missing Mass",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 4,
          "slideType": "hook | concept",
          "slideTitle": "Hook: A Scientific Mystery",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO3"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Scientific Inquiry & Conservation of Mass"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "To capture attention with a paradox or real-world problem that requires scientific reasoning to solve.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Science is about solving puzzles using evidence, not just memorizing rules.",
          "layout": "Story/Problem Statement",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Scenario: A log burns completely in a sealed container.",
            "Observation: The ash weighs less than the original log.",
            "Question: Where did the missing mass go?",
            "Challenge: Use evidence to explain."
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "The Mystery:",
            "- A heavy log burns in a sealed box.",
            "- Only light ash remains inside.",
            "- Did matter disappear? Or change form?",
            "Think-Pair-Share: What happened?"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Imagine this: You weigh a large log. It's 10kg. You burn it completely in a sealed glass box so nothing escapes.",
            "Afterward, you only have ash left inside the box weighing maybe 2kg. Where did the other 8kg go?",
            "This is a classic scientific mystery that led to our understanding of conservation of mass and gases.",
            "Discuss with your partner: Did matter disappear? Or did it change into something invisible like gas?",
            "Transition: This leads us directly into how we define and classify the world around us."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": null,
          "visualPlan": "Illustration of a sealed glass box containing burning wood/ash with weight scales showing before and after.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visualizing the conservation of mass experiment",
              "searchQuery": "science experiment sealed container burning log ash weighing scale educational illustration",
              "sourceSite": "Google Imagen API",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Internally generated or Creative Commons",
              "attributionText": "Conservation of Mass Experiment",
              "altText": "Diagram showing a sealed container with burning wood and ash, illustrating mass conservation.",
              "placementHint": "Left panel"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Animate the scale dropping to show weight change."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 4
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "topic_introduction",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Core Concepts: Matter, Energy & Systems",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 5,
          "slideType": "concept | summary",
          "slideTitle": "The Building Blocks of Science",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO1"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Matter and Energy",
            "Systems Thinking"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "To formally introduce the scope of today's revision, covering the interconnected nature of scientific concepts.",
          "studentTakeaway": "I understand that matter changes form but is conserved, energy drives processes, and systems are connected.",
          "layout": "Definition & Context",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Matter: Anything with mass and volume (Solid, Liquid, Gas)",
            "Energy: The ability to do work or cause change",
            "Systems: Interconnected parts working together (e.g., Ecosystems)",
            "Key Principle: Conservation of Mass & Energy"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Matter: Solid, Liquid, Gas - Changes form but not lost.",
            "Energy: Drives all processes in nature and machines.",
            "Systems: Parts interacting (e.g., Food Chains).",
            "Golden Rule: Matter & Energy are conserved."
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Let's define our terms clearly. Matter is everything that has mass—solids, liquids, gases.",
            "Energy is what makes things happen; it powers the sun, your heart beating, and chemical reactions.",
            "We view these through 'Systems'—like an ecosystem where plants (biology) use sunlight (physics/energy) to grow.",
            "Remember our mystery? The mass didn't disappear because of this Golden Rule: Conservation of Mass & Energy.",
            "Transition: Now let's visualize how matter changes states and interacts."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": null,
          "visualPlan": "A diagram showing the three states of matter (solid, liquid, gas) with particles arranged differently.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visualizing states of matter",
              "searchQuery": "states of matter solid liquid gas particle arrangement educational diagram",
              "sourceSite": "Google Imagen API",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Internally generated or Creative Commons",
              "attributionText": "States of Matter Diagram",
              "altText": "Diagram showing particles in solid, liquid, and gas states.",
              "placementHint": "Center panel"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Show particle movement increasing from solid to gas."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 4
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "core_concept_a",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Concept A: Forces and Motion Dynamics",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 6,
          "slideType": "concept | comparison",
          "slideTitle": "Forces & Motion: The Physics of Movement",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO1"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Physics - Forces and Motion"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "To review the fundamental laws governing motion, emphasizing cause-and-effect relationships.",
          "studentTakeaway": "I can explain how forces like friction and gravity affect movement in everyday life.",
          "layout": "Text + Formula/Principle Box",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Newton's Laws: Inertia, F=ma, Action-Reaction",
            "Friction: Opposes motion between surfaces",
            "Gravity: Pulls objects toward Earth (9.8 m/s²)",
            "Net Force determines acceleration"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "- Newton's 1st Law: Objects stay at rest or in motion unless acted upon.",
            "- F = ma: Force equals mass times acceleration.",
            "- Friction & Gravity are key opposing forces.",
            "- Net Force causes change in velocity."
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Let's dive into Physics. Newton's laws explain why we need seatbelts (inertia) and how rockets launch (action-reaction).",
            "Remember F=ma? If you push a heavy truck, it accelerates slowly because of its large mass.",
            "Friction is the force that stops your bike when you stop pedaling. Gravity pulls everything down at 9.8 m/s².",
            "The key takeaway: Net Force determines if an object speeds up, slows down, or changes direction.",
            "Transition: Now let's look at a visual representation of how these forces interact in a system."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": null,
          "visualPlan": "Diagram showing a block being pushed with arrows indicating applied force, friction, gravity, and normal force.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Force diagram visualization",
              "searchQuery": "free body diagram forces on an object physics education vector arrows",
              "sourceSite": "Google Imagen API",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Internally generated or Creative Commons",
              "attributionText": "Free Body Diagram",
              "altText": "Diagram showing vectors for force, friction, gravity on an object.",
              "placementHint": "Right panel"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Animate arrows to show direction and relative magnitude."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 5
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "core_concept_b_visual",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Concept B: Biological Systems & Energy Flow",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 7,
          "slideType": "process | comparison",
          "slideTitle": "Biological Systems: The Web of Life",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO1"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Biology - Ecosystems and Energy Flow"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "To use a visual model to explain the flow of energy through ecosystems, connecting biology with physics concepts.",
          "studentTakeaway": "I understand that energy flows in one direction (sun -> producers -> consumers) while matter cycles.",
          "layout": "Diagram / Flowchart",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "- Producers capture solar energy (Photosynthesis)",
            "- Consumers eat for energy transfer",
            "- Decomposers recycle nutrients back to soil",
            "- Energy decreases at each trophic level"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Energy Flow: Sun -> Plants -> Animals -> Heat Loss",
            "Matter Cycle: Nutrients recycled by decomposers.",
            "Trophic Levels: 10% energy transfer rule.",
            "Interdependence: All parts rely on each other."
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Now let's switch to Biology. Look at this food web diagram.",
            "Energy starts with the sun, captured by plants (producers). When animals eat them, energy transfers up.",
            "Notice that only about 10% of energy moves to the next level; the rest is lost as heat or used for life processes.",
            "Unlike energy which flows one way, matter cycles. Decomposers break down dead things and return nutrients to the soil for plants again.",
            "This connects back to our physics: Energy transformation follows conservation laws too!",
            "Transition: Let's see how we apply this knowledge in a practical scenario."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": "A detailed food web diagram showing producers, primary consumers, secondary consumers, and decomposers with arrows indicating energy flow.",
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Food Web Diagram: Sun -> Grass (Producer) -> Rabbit (Primary Consumer) -> Fox (Secondary Consumer) -> Decomposer. Arrows show direction of energy transfer.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visualizing the food web and energy flow",
              "searchQuery": "food web diagram educational illustration sun grass rabbit fox decomposers arrows energy flow",
              "sourceSite": "Google Imagen API",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Internally generated or Creative Commons",
              "attributionText": "Food Web Diagram",
              "altText": "Diagram illustrating the food web and energy transfer between trophic levels.",
              "placementHint": "Full slide background"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Highlight arrows sequentially to show flow."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 5
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "worked_example",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Worked Example: Analyzing Experimental Data",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 8,
          "slideType": "process | summary",
          "slideTitle": "Example: Interpreting a Growth Graph",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO2"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Data Interpretation & Scientific Method"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "To model how to analyze data, identify trends, and draw evidence-based conclusions.",
          "studentTakeaway": "I can look at a graph, describe the trend, and explain what it means in real-world terms.",
          "layout": "Graph + Step-by-Step Analysis",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "- Observation: Plant height increases over time.",
            "- Trend: Rapid growth initially, then slows down (Plateau).",
            "- Inference: Nutrient depletion or light limitation occurred.",
            "- Conclusion: Growth is limited by environmental factors."
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Graph Analysis:",
            "- X-axis: Time (Days)",
            "- Y-axis: Plant Height (cm)",
            "- Trend: Rapid rise, then plateau at Day 14.",
            "- Conclusion: Growth limited by resources after Day 10."
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Let's practice interpreting data. Look at this graph of plant growth over two weeks.",
            "First, what do the axes represent? Time and Height.",
            "What is the trend? Rapid increase for the first week, then it levels off (plateaus).",
            "Why did it stop growing so fast? We infer that resources like nutrients or light became limiting factors around Day 10-12.",
            "This is how scientists use data to make conclusions about real-world constraints. Always look for patterns and limits!",
            "Transition: Now you will try this analysis yourself."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": "Line graph showing plant height increasing over 14 days, with a plateau after day 10.",
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Line Graph: X-axis (Days 0-14), Y-axis (Height cm). Curve rises steeply then flattens. Annotated points at Day 5 and Day 12 showing growth rate change.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Data visualization for analysis practice",
              "searchQuery": "line graph plant growth over time educational chart plateau trend line",
              "sourceSite": "Google Imagen API",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Internally generated or Creative Commons",
              "attributionText": "Plant Growth Graph",
              "altText": "Line graph showing plant growth over 14 days with a plateau.",
              "placementHint": "Left panel"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Draw the line step-by-step as you explain."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 5
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "guided_practice",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Guided Practice: Design Your Experiment",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 9,
          "slideType": "process | summary",
          "slideTitle": "Activity: Plan a Scientific Investigation",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO3"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Scientific Inquiry & Experimental Design"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "To have students actively apply the scientific method by designing an experiment, fostering critical thinking.",
          "studentTakeaway": "I can design a simple experiment with variables and controls to test a hypothesis.",
          "layout": "Activity Instructions + Prompt",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "- Task: Design an experiment on 'Light & Plant Growth'.",
            "- Identify Variables: Independent (Light), Dependent (Height).",
            "- Control Group: Plants in normal light.",
            "- Experimental Group: Plants in low/dark conditions."
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Your Turn:",
            "- Hypothesis: What happens if plants get no light?",
            "- Variables: Identify Independent & Dependent variables.",
            "- Control: Keep everything else constant (Water, Soil).",
            "- Prediction: Will they grow? Why?"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Now it's your turn to be scientists. Imagine you want to test how light affects plant growth.",
            "What is the one thing you change? That's your Independent Variable (Light intensity).",
            "What are you measuring? The Dependent Variable (Plant height/health).",
            "Remember, a control group gets normal conditions so we have something to compare against.",
            "Discuss with your partner: What would happen if the plant got no light at all?",
            "Transition: Let's check what everyone has learned."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": null,
          "visualPlan": "Icons representing a hypothesis question mark, variables (light bulb), and control group comparison.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual cues for experimental design",
              "searchQuery": "scientific method icons experiment design light bulb plant growth educational symbols",
              "sourceSite": "Google Imagen API",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Internally generated or Creative Commons",
              "attributionText": "Experimental Design Icons",
              "altText": "Icons representing hypothesis, variables, and control groups.",
              "placementHint": "Right panel"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Reveal each step of the design process."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 6
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "quick_assessment",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Quick Assessment: Check Your Understanding",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 10,
          "slideType": "summary | concept",
          "slideTitle": "Assessment: Test Your Knowledge",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO2"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Formative Assessment"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "To quickly gauge student understanding of the key concepts covered in this session.",
          "studentTakeaway": "I can answer questions about data interpretation and scientific principles to verify my learning.",
          "layout": "Question Cards",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "- Q1: What is the unit of Force?",
            "- Q2: In a food web, where does energy start?",
            "- Q3: Why did the plant growth plateau in our graph?"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Quick Fire Questions:",
            "1. Unit of Force? (A) Joule (B) Newton",
            "2. Energy source for producers? (A) Soil (B) Sunlight",
            "3. Why did growth stop in the graph?",
            "- A: Nutrient depletion / Limiting factor"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Let's do a quick check to see how we're doing.",
            "Question 1: What is the unit of Force? Is it Joule or Newton? (Answer: Newton).",
            "Question 2: Where does energy start for producers in a food web? Soil or Sunlight? (Sunlight!).",
            "Question 3: Why did our plant growth plateau? Because resources became limited.",
            "These questions test your ability to recall facts and interpret data trends. Great job so far!",
            "Transition: Let's wrap up what we've learned today."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": null,
          "visualPlan": "Simple question cards or a quiz interface layout with options.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual representation of assessment questions",
              "searchQuery": "quiz format science education multiple choice icons checkmark wrong answer educational graphic",
              "sourceSite": "Google Imagen API",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Internally generated or Creative Commons",
              "attributionText": "Assessment Graphics",
              "altText": "Visuals representing quiz questions and answers.",
              "placementHint": "Center panel"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Reveal question then answer on click."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 4
        },
        {
          "templateId": "kamalaniketan-session-12",
          "templateSlideKey": "summary",
